topic: An employee is asked to train their replacement
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the conference room hummed, a grating soundtrack to my rising unease. Sarah, the department head, leaned forward, her expression one of practiced, gentle encouragement. “Mark, I need you to take the lead on onboarding Alex. You know the systems inside and out.” I shifted in my chair, the worn leather protesting under my weight. My gaze drifted to the stack of manuals on the table—thick, intimidating binders that seemed designed to induce panic. *Another project, another person who thinks they know better,* I thought, the feeling settling cold in my stomach. I forced a nod, my throat suddenly dry. “Right. I can handle that.” Inside, I cataloged every potential pitfall, every awkward question Alex might ask, already mapping out scenarios where things would inevitably go wrong. It felt less like training and more like being tasked with babysitting a novice.

[story 2]

“This is your chance to really step up, David,” Mr. Henderson boomed, clapping his hands together with an alarming enthusiasm. He gestured toward the new hire, a young man named Ben, who was currently looking pale and clutching his notepad like a lifeline. I watched the exchange, my arms crossed tightly over my chest. A small, involuntary tightening in my jaw betrayed the churning in my gut. *I’ve done this before. I’ve taught people. Why am I suddenly the designated guru?* When Sarah finally stepped away, leaving just Ben and me, I cleared my throat. “Look, Ben,” I began, my voice deliberately even, trying to project confidence I absolutely did not feel. “It’s all a process. We’ll go through it step-by-step. Don't expect perfection on day one.” I noticed Ben’s eyes flicking up to mine, a flicker of doubt crossing his features, and I immediately felt a defensive wall go up around my composure.

[story 3]

I meticulously organized my desk, pushing stray papers into perfect alignment. The air in the office felt thick with unspoken expectations. When Maria finally called me in, her tone was brisk, almost impatient. “I need you to mentor Chloe. She’s new, and I need you to make sure she gets up to speed quickly.” I stopped fiddling with my pen. My hands, usually steady, started to tremble slightly as I looked at the calendar—a densely packed schedule of unfamiliar procedures. *This is going to be a nightmare of repetition,* I thought, a wave of weary resignation washing over me. I met her gaze, trying to project an aura of calm competence, but I could feel the resistance building beneath my skin. “I’ll review the documentation,” I managed, my voice sounding flatter than I intended. “We can schedule a check-in every afternoon.” It sounded like a concession, not a commitment.
